revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[arz:هيراتا اتسوتان]][[de:Hirata Atsutane]][[en:Hirata Atsutane]][[fa:هیراتا آتسوتانه]][[fr:Hirata Atsutane]][[id:Hirata Atsutane]][[it:Hirata Atsutane]][[ja:平田篤胤]][[ko:히라타 아쓰타네]][[pl:Atsutane Hirata]][[pt:Hirata Atsutane]][[ru:Хирата Ацутанэ]][[sl:Hirata Acutane]][[sv:Hirata Atsutane]][[tr:Hirata Atsutane]][[vi:Hirata Atsutane]][[zh:平田笃胤]]
{{Infobox person|image=File:Portrait of Atsutane Hirata.jpg|birth_date={{birth date|1776|10|6}}|death_date={{death date|1843|11|2}}|birth_place={{ill|Akita|qid=Q17139|1=Akita (city)}}|gender={{ill|male|qid=Q6581097|1=Male (gender)}}|nationality={{ill|Japan|qid=Q17|1=Japan}}|occupation={{ill|linguist|qid=Q14467526|1=Linguist}}; {{ill|philosopher|qid=Q4964182|1=Philosopher}}; {{ill|theologian|qid=Q1234713|1=Theologian}}|notable_works={{ill|Ino mononokeroku|qid=Q11596744|1=Ino mononokeroku}}; {{ill|Q11410975|qid=Q11410975|1=Q11410975}}; {{ill|Q11410978|qid=Q11410978|1=Q11410978}}; {{ill|Q11589447|qid=Q11589447|1=Q11589447}}; {{ill|Q60986582|qid=Q60986582|1=Q60986582}}|religion={{ill|Fukko Shinto|qid=Q11489378|1=Fukko Shinto}}|organization={{ill|Four Great Men of Kokugaku|qid=Q29915315|1=Four Great Men of Kokugaku}}|native_name=平田 篤胤|resting_place={{ill|Tegata|qid=Q11497034|1=Tegata}}}}

{{Short description|18th and 19th-century Japanese theologian}}
{{Expand Japanese|topic=bio|date=January 2022}}
{{Infobox philosopher
| name          = Hirata Atsutane
| image         = Hirata Atsutane Jigazo.jpg
| caption       = Self-portrait by Hirata, made in 1841 at the age of 65.
| birth_name    = {{Nihongo|'''Ōwada Taneyuki'''|大和田胤行}}
| birth_date    = {{Birth date|1776|10|06}}
| birth_place   = {{ill|Kubota Domain|en|Kubota Domain|qid=Q1067720|lt=Kubota Domain|1=Kubota Domain}}, {{ill|Dewa Province|en|Dewa Province|qid=Q876032|lt=Dewa Province|1=Dewa Province}}, [[Tokugawa shogunate|Japan]]
| death_date    = {{Death date and age|1843|11|02|1776|10|06}} 
| death_place   = Kubota Domain, Dewa Province, Japan
| nationality   = Japanese
| other_names   =
| occupation    = 
| years_active  = 
| known_for     = 
| notable_works = 
| influences    = {{hlist|[[Hayashi Razan|Razan]]|{{ill|Yamazaki Ansai|en|Yamazaki Ansai|qid=Q6565636|lt=Ansai|1=Yamazaki Ansai}}|[[Ge Hong|Ge]]|{{ill|Zhuang Zhou|en|Zhuang Zhou|qid=Q47739|lt=Zhuangzi|1=Zhuang Zhou}}|{{ill|Motoori Norinaga|en|Motoori Norinaga|qid=Q384647|lt=Motoori|1=Motoori Norinaga}}|{{ill|Nicolaus Copernicus|en|Nicolaus Copernicus|qid=Q619|lt=Copernicus|1=Nicolaus Copernicus}}|[[Kamo no Mabuchi|Kamo]]|[[Kada no Azumamaro|Kada]]|[[Keichū]]|[[Matteo Ricci|Ricci]]}}
| influenced    = {{hlist|{{ill|Hayashi Ōen|en|Hayashi Ōen|qid=Q5686042|lt=Ōen|1=Hayashi Ōen}}}}
}}
{{family name hatnote|Hirata|lang=Japanese}}
{{Nihongo|'''Hirata Atsutane'''|平田 篤胤||6 October 1776 – 2 November 1843}} was a Japanese scholar, conventionally Ranked as one of the {{ill|Four Great Men of Kokugaku|en|Four Great Men of Kokugaku|qid=Q29915315|lt=Four Great Men of Kokugaku|1=Four Great Men of Kokugaku}} (nativist) studies,<ref>"Hirata Atsutane (1776-1843) is best known as the fourth Great Man of ''kokugaku''." Hansen, Wilburn. "Dancing as if Possessed: A Coming Out Party in Edo Spirit Society." ''Japanese Journal of Religious Studies'' (2010): pp 275-294, of which page 277, footnote 3.</ref> and one of the most significant {{ill|Theology|en|Theology|qid=Q34178|lt=theologians|1=Theology}} of the [[Shinto|Shintō]] religion. His literary name was {{Nihongo|'''Ibukinoya'''|気吹舎}}, and his primary assumed name was {{Nihongo|'''Daigaku'''|大壑|Great Abyss}}{{ref|a}}. He also used the names {{Nihongo|'''Daikaku'''|大角}}, {{Nihongo|'''Gentaku'''|玄琢}}, and {{Nihongo|'''Genzui'''|玄瑞}}. His [[Japanese name#Historical names|personal name]] was {{Nihongo|'''Hanbē'''|半兵衛}}.

==Biography==
===Early life===
Hirata was born as the fourth son of {{Nihongo|Ōwada Seihē Toshitane|大和田 清兵衛 祚胤}}, an ''Obangashira'' (low-Ranking) ''{{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|qid=Q38142|lt=Samurai|1=Samurai}}'' of {{ill|Kubota Domain|en|Kubota Domain|qid=Q1067720|lt=Kubota Domain|1=Kubota Domain}}, in what is now part of the city of {{ill|Akita (city)|en|Akita (city)|qid=Q17139|lt=Akita|1=Akita (city)}} in northern Japan. Little is known of his early childhood, but it appears he was impoverished and faced hostility from his step-mother. He left home in 1795, renouncing his ties to his family and to the Domain and traveled to {{ill|Edo|en|Edo|qid=Q215646|lt=Edo|1=Edo}} and worked as a laborer and as a servant, while pushing opportunities to study ''{{ill|Rangaku|en|Rangaku|qid=Q877637|lt=Rangaku|1=Rangaku}}'', {{ill|Geography|en|Geography|qid=Q1071|lt=Geography|1=Geography}}, and {{ill|Astronomy|en|Astronomy|qid=Q333|lt=Astronomy|1=Astronomy}}. In 1800, at the age of 25, he caught the attention of Hirata Tōbē (平田藤兵衛), a scholar and instructor in the {{ill|Yamaga Sokō|en|Yamaga Sokō|qid=Q1095270|lt=Yamaga school|1=Yamaga Sokō}} of military strategy, formerly of {{ill|Bitchū-Matsuyama Domain|en|Bitchū-Matsuyama Domain|qid=Q3034422|lt=Bitchū-Matsuyama Domain|1=Bitchū-Matsuyama Domain}}, who adopted him and from whom he received the family name of Hirata. Around this time, he met his future wife, the daughter of Ishibashi Tsunefusa (石橋常房), a ''{{ill|Hatamoto|en|Hatamoto|qid=Q572662|lt=Hatamoto|1=Hatamoto}}'' of {{ill|Numazu Domain|en|Numazu Domain|qid=Q1005450|lt=Numazu Domain|1=Numazu Domain}}, whom he married the following year. Evidently the two became romantically involved while Hirata was working as a menial servant in the Ishibashi household.<ref>{{cite web |title=2004 Hirata Atsutane Exhibition|url=https://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/about/hitoiki/break5/break5.html |website=[[National Museum of Japanese History]] |access-date=10 January 2022}}</ref> Taking his family name, she was known as Hirata Orise (平田織瀬).

===Development of Kokugaku studies===
[[File:Portrait of Atsutane Hirata.jpg|thumb|200px|Portrait of Atsutane Hirata, from an 18th century hanging scroll by an unknown author.]]
While in Edo, Hirata was a student of the {{ill|Neo-Confucianism|en|Neo-Confucianism|qid=Q825754|lt=Neo-Confucianism|1=Neo-Confucianism}} of {{ill|Yamazaki Ansai|en|Yamazaki Ansai|qid=Q6565636|lt=Yamazaki Ansai|1=Yamazaki Ansai}} (1619–1682); however, his interests were very broad. Concerned by {{ill|Russian Empire|en|Russian Empire|qid=Q34266|lt=Russian|1=Russian Empire}} raids and incursions into north {{ill|Ezo|en|Ezo|qid=Q1040184|lt=Ezo|1=Ezo}} (today's {{ill|Sakhalin|en|Sakhalin|qid=Q7792|lt=Sakhalin|1=Sakhalin}} and the {{ill|Kuril Islands|en|Kuril Islands|qid=Q80389|lt=Kuril Islands|1=Kuril Islands}}), he studied modern {{ill|Netherlands|en|Netherlands|qid=Q55|lt=Dutch|1=Netherlands}} medicine under the surgeon {{illm|Yoshida Chōshuku|ja|吉田長淑}}. His studies under Yoshida included at least one human dissection.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Miyachi |first1=Masato |title=Bakumatsu ishin henkakushi, Volume 1 |date=August 2012 |publisher=Iwanami Shoten |isbn=9784000244688 |edition=1st}}</ref> He later turned towards [[:en:Daoism|Daoism]] as found in the works of the Chinese philosopher {{ill|Zhuang Zhou|en|Zhuang Zhou|qid=Q47739|lt=Zhuangzi|1=Zhuang Zhou}}. He learned about the works of {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|en|Motoori Norinaga|qid=Q384647|lt=Motoori Norinaga|1=Motoori Norinaga}}, the founder of the ''{{ill|Kokugaku|en|Kokugaku|qid=Q855159|lt=Kokugaku|1=Kokugaku}}'' movement, in 1803,  two years after Motoori's death. Hirata claimed later to have received the mantle of ''kokugaku'' teacher in a dream directly from Motoori Norinaga, but the story is apocryphal. Originally, he published under the name of {{Nihongo|'''Masuganoya'''|真菅乃屋}}, meaning "house of {{ill|Cyperaceae|en|Cyperaceae|qid=Q155843|lt=sedge|1=Cyperaceae}}", but during a tour of rural shrines in 1816 he acquired an ancient {{ill|Xun (instrument)|en|Xun (instrument)|qid=Q839767|lt=stone vessel flute|1=Xun (instrument)}} which he cherished dearly and changed his publishing name to Ibukinoya, or "house of breath". He became a student of {{ill|Motoori Haruniwa|en|Motoori Haruniwa|qid=Q6537795|lt=Motoori Haruniwa|1=Motoori Haruniwa}}, and read voraciously the ancient and Chinese classics, foreign works by {{ill|Nicolaus Copernicus|en|Nicolaus Copernicus|qid=Q619|lt=Nicolaus Copernicus|1=Nicolaus Copernicus}} and {{ill|Isaac Newton|en|Isaac Newton|qid=Q935|lt=Isaac Newton|1=Isaac Newton}} and treatises on Buddhism and Shinto. He was also a prolific writer. Representative works in the study of ancient Japanese traditions include ''Tama no mihashira'', ''Koshi seibun'', ''Kodō taii'' and ''Zoku shintō taii'', and the commentaries ''Koshichō'' and ''Koshiden''. He is also noted for his studies of ancient Indian and Chinese tradition (''Indo zōshi'' and ''Morokoshi taikoden''), and texts dealing with the spirit world, including ''Senkyō ibun'' and ''Katsugorō saisei kibun''. His early work ''Honkyō gaihen'' indicates an acquaintance with Christian literature that had been authored by Jesuits in China. Orise died in 1812.

Though he is traditionally Ranked fourth in the lineage of ''kokugaku'' scholars, Hirata actually represents a break with the purely scholarly urban culture characteristic of the revival of classical nativist learning, and represents a trend toward a populist message. Hirata laid particular emphasis on reaching the average man, and adapted his own style to them by employing at times the vernacular idiom. Hirata frequently expressed hostility to the Confucian and Buddhist scholars of the day, advocating instead a revival of the “ancient ways” in which the {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|Imperial House of Japan|qid=Q909452|lt=emperor|1=Imperial House of Japan}} was to be revered. Hirata's first published work, ''Kamōsho'' (1803) was a scathing attack on the works of Confucian philosopher [[Dazai Shundai]] (1680–1747)  on Buddhism, and resulted in an invitation to teach from the Yoshida family, the hereditary clan leading [[:en:Yoshida Shinto|Yoshida Shinto]].

The contents of his 1841 treatise ''Tenchō mukyūreki'', in which he suggested that loyalty to the Emperor ought to take precedence over loyalty to one's lord (i.e. the {{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|qid=Q131767|lt=Shogun|1=Shogun}}), angered the ruling [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa government]], and he was sentenced to {{ill|House arrest|en|House arrest|qid=Q320994|lt=House arrest|1=House arrest}} in Akita until his death in 1843.

==Influence==
{{Conservatism in Japan|Intellectuals}}
Hirata's activities eventually attracted over 500 pupils, including [[Okuni Takamasa]] and {{ill|Suzuki Shigetane|en|Suzuki Shigetane|qid=Q20040844|lt=Suzuki Shigetane|1=Suzuki Shigetane}}. His {{ill|Japanese nationalism|en|Japanese nationalism|qid=Q1331440|lt=nationalist|1=Japanese nationalism}} writings had considerable impact on the ''samurai'' who supported the ''[[Sonnō jōi]]'' movement and who fought in the {{ill|Boshin War|en|Boshin War|qid=Q309184|lt=Boshin War|1=Boshin War}} to overthrow the Tokugawa Shogunate during the [[Meiji Restoration]].

Hirata's influence on ''kokugaku'' has recently been thought to be overestimated. While he is called one of the "four great men of kokugaku", this is a phrase he invented himself.  His work more often influenced religious groups than the government in the {{ill|Empire of Japan|en|Empire of Japan|qid=Q188712|lt=Empire of Japan|1=Empire of Japan}}.

Among Hirata's more enduring contributions to Japanese thought was to remind that all Japanese were descended from the [[kami]], not only the Imperial family and certain aristocratic families. As he put it, "this, our glorious land, is the land in which the kami have their origin, and we are one and all descendants of the kami. For this reason, if we go back from the parents who gave us life and being, beyond the grandparents and great-grandparents, and consider the ancestors of ancient times, then the original ancestors of those must necessarily have been the kami."<ref>Earl, David Margarey, Emperor and Nation in Japan, Political Thinkers of the Tokugawa Period, University of Washington Press, 1964, p. 80</ref>

==Hirata Atsutane's grave==

Hirata died at his home in Nakakame-cho, Akita in 1843, and as per the provisions of his will, was buried on a hillside in the city. {{coord|39.72863931|140.1372221|format=dms|display=inline}} The tombstone is a natural stone simply inscribed with his name, surrounded by a stone fence and a stone ''[[torii]]'' gate are placed at the entrance. The tomb was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1934.<ref>{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/209560|title=平田篤胤墓|language=ja |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] }}</ref>

==Selected works==

* {{Nihongo|''Chishima no shiranami''|千島の白波|White Waves of {{ill|Kuril Islands|en|Kuril Islands|qid=Q80389|lt=Chishima|1=Kuril Islands}}}}{{ref|b}}
* {{Nihongo|''Honkyō gaihen''|本教外篇|Outer Chapters of Our Doctrine}}
* {{Nihongo|''Kodō taii''|古道大意|General Introduction to the Path of Antiquity}}
* {{Nihongo|''Tama no mihashira''|霊能真柱|True Pillar of the Spirit}}
* {{Nihongo|''Tamadasuki''|玉襷|The Precious {{ill|Tasuki (sash)|en|Tasuki (sash)|qid=Q10856347|lt=Tasuki|1=Tasuki (sash)}}}}
* {{Nihongo|''Zoku shintō taii''|俗神道大意|General Introduction to Folk Shintō}}
* {{Nihongo|''Koshichō''|古史徴|Index to Ancient History}} 
* {{Nihongo|''Koshiden''|古史伝|Commentary on Ancient History}}
* {{Nihongo|''Shizu no iwaya''|志都能石屋|The Quiet Cave}}
* {{Nihongo|''Shutsujō shōgo''|出定笑語|Laughing Discourse upon Emerging from Meditation}}{{ref|c}}
* {{Nihongo|''Indo zōshi''|印度蔵志|Collection of Truth About India}}
* {{Nihongo|''Morokoshi taikoden''|唐土太古傳|Transmission of Ancient Chinese History}}
* {{Nihongo|''Senkyō ibun''|仙境異聞|Strange Tales from the Land of the Immortals}}
* {{Nihongo|''Katsugorō saisei kibun''|勝五郎再生記聞|Account of Katsugorō's Rebirth}}
* {{Nihongo|''Reihō gogaku shinkeizu''|霊宝五嶽真形図|Accurate Diagram of the Five Peaks}}
* {{Nihongo|''Saiseki gairon''|西籍慨論|Reprobation of Western Scholarship}}
* {{Nihongo|''Tenchūki''|天柱記|Record of the Pillar of Heaven}}
* {{Nihongo|''Kamōsho''|呵妄書|Rebuke of Insolent Writings}}
* {{Nihongo|''Kokon yōmikō''|古今妖魅考|Investigation of Paranormal Phenomena of the Past and Present}}
* {{Nihongo|''Tenchō mukyūreki''|天朝無窮暦|Calendar of the Immortal Imperial Court}}
* A commentary on the {{Nihongo|''Inō mononokeroku''|稲生物怪録|Account of the Haunting of Inō}} of 1749.

==Notes==
:1.{{note|a}}The name was inspired by a passge from the {{ill|Zhuangzi (book)|en|Zhuangzi (book)|qid=Q1074987|lt=Zhuangzi|1=Zhuangzi (book)}}. Although the term is today generally translated as "ocean", its use in classical Chinese literature with which Hirata had intimate familiarity included a more general meaning of vast profundity and was used metaphorically with regard to {{ill|Qi|en|Qi|qid=Q202742|lt=Qi|1=Qi}} and {{ill|Dantian|en|Dantian|qid=Q1165048|lt=its source|1=Dantian}}. "Zhun Mang (諄芒), on his way to the ocean, met with Yuan Feng on the shore of the eastern sea, and was asked by him where he was going. 'I am going,' he replied, 'to the ocean;' and the other again asked, 'What for?' Zhun Mang said, 'Such is the nature of the ocean (大壑, dàhè) that the waters which flow into it can never fill it, nor those which flow from it exhaust it. I will enjoy myself, rambling by it.'"
:2.{{note|b}}A treatise on national defense advocating a military buildup on Sakhalin and throughout the Kuriles to counter Russian expansion into {{ill|Northeast Asia|en|Northeast Asia|qid=Q27243|lt=Northeast Asia|1=Northeast Asia}}.<ref>{{cite book | last = Hansen | first = Wilburn | year = 2008 | title = When Tengu Talk: Hirata Atsutane's Ethnography of the Other World | publisher = University of Hawai‘i Press | isbn = 9780824832094}}</ref>
:3.{{note|c}}This text's name is a pun on the strongly anti-Shinto {{ill|Tominaga Nakamoto|en|Tominaga Nakamoto|qid=Q4389511|lt=Tominaga Nakamoto|1=Tominaga Nakamoto}}'s 1745 {{illm|Shutsujō gogo|ja|出定後語}}, or "Discourse upon Emerging from Meditation".

==See also==

}} {{ISBN|9780195110609}}; {{ISBN|9780195110616}}; [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/49704795  OCLC 49704795]
